name: Cesky Terrier
description: The Cesky Terrier is one of the world's rarest dog breeds, developed by Czech geneticist Frantisek Horak in 1948 by crossing the Scottish Terrier with the Sealyham Terrier to create a calmer, more cooperative working terrier for hunting in Bohemian forests. Unlike most terriers, the Cesky is notably more easygoing and trainable, making it a more suitable companion for families and less experienced dog owners. Their silky coat requires professional grooming and clipping, but they are low shedders and relatively adaptable.

conditions_detail.name: Scottie Cramp
conditions_detail.description: Scottie cramp is a hereditary episodic movement disorder in Scottish Terriers caused by a serotonin metabolism defect. Episodes of muscle over-contraction occur during excitement or exercise, causing abnormal gait and posture but no pain or loss of consciousness.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Diazepam or acepromazine may reduce episode frequency and severity. Avoiding triggers is the primary management. The condition is not progressive and does not shorten lifespan.
prevention_tips: DNA test available — avoid breeding affected dogs
Reduce exercise intensity and excitement if needed

conditions_detail.name: Lens Luxation
conditions_detail.description: Lens luxation is displacement of the lens from its normal position due to breakdown of the suspensory ligaments (zonules). Primary lens luxation is hereditary. A luxated lens can cause acute glaucoma and retinal detachment.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Surgical removal of the luxated lens (intracapsular extraction) is the treatment of choice. Medical management with miotics helps when surgery is not immediately possible. Glaucoma treatment may also be required.
prevention_tips: DNA test available (ADAMTS17 mutation) — do not breed affected dogs
